currentUse: Active worship as Jerusalem Lutheran Church (LCMS), 305 Collinsville Ave — weekly Sunday service at 10:00 AM
summary: The parish survives as Jerusalem Lutheran Church at 305 Collinsville Ave, Collinsville, IL — the small wooden church the Lithuanian Lutheran congregation built in 1903 still stands and still holds weekly worship. The congregation was organized January 25, 1903 by Lithuanian immigrants who came to work Collinsville's coal mines, with the knygnešys pastor Martynas Keturakaitis (who had served a prison term for smuggling Lithuanian books under the Russian ban) as its first pastor. It is one of only three Lithuanian Lutheran churches in the USA, and its stained glass windows still carry the Lithuanian names of the original sponsors, including the surname Shimkus — U.S. Rep. John Shimkus's grandparents were members, and for the 2003 centennial he arranged a visit from Lithuania's ambassador Vygaudas Usackas. The congregation today is a member of the Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod (Southern Illinois District, V-Circuit): the 2025 LCMS locator lists Rev. Dr. Douglas A. Nicely as sole pastor, Sunday worship at 10:00 AM with Sunday School and Bible class at 9:00 AM, average weekly attendance of 21, and 27 baptized / 26 confirmed members. In 2017 the congregation installed a new Rodgers Classic Series 559 organ, dedicated November 19, 2017 with music reflecting its Lithuanian Lutheran heritage. The congregation's Facebook page showed activity as recently as February 2025. It remains a very small, aging congregation, but there has been no closure, sale, or demolition — the building has never left the congregation's hands.

developments.headline: Centennial celebrated with visit from Lithuania's ambassador
developments.detail: For the 100th anniversary in April 2003, U.S. Rep. John Shimkus (R-Collinsville), whose grandparents had been members of the church, arranged a visit from the ambassador of Lithuania, Vygaudas Usackas. The church has been recognized as a historical landmark by the Collinsville Historical Society.

sources.title: More than a century of dedication (via search snippet; full article paywalled)

developments.headline: New Rodgers organ installed and dedicated with Lithuanian-heritage program
developments.detail: The congregation acquired a Rodgers Classic Series 559 organ through donations; it was installed and voiced August 25, 2017, first used in worship August 27, and dedicated November 19, 2017 with hymnody reflecting the congregation's Lithuanian Lutheran heritage. Pastor Doug Nicely led the church; Brian Keller was congregation president.

sources.title: A Musical Blessing at Jerusalem Lutheran Church with a New Classic Series 559 Organ!

developments.headline: Still active: LCMS locator lists weekly worship, pastor, and 2025 membership stats
developments.detail: The official LCMS locator (2025 statistics) lists Jerusalem Lutheran Church, 305 Collinsville Ave, as an active congregation of the Southern Illinois District (V-Circuit 004): Rev. Dr. Douglas A. Nicely, sole pastor; Sunday worship 10:00 AM, Sunday School and Bible class 9:00 AM; average weekly attendance 21, baptized membership 27, confirmed membership 26; food and community ministries. Organized December 31, 1903. The church's Facebook page showed a post as recently as February 2, 2025.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: One material refinement: the archive record describes the parish as an "independent self-governing Lithuanian Lutheran congregation... independent of any diocese," but the congregation is in fact a member of the Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod (Southern Illinois District) — the LCMS locator states it joined the LCMS in 1903, and Global True Lithuania documents a post-WWII dispute between older members favoring Missouri Synod affiliation and DP-era refugees favoring the Lithuanian Lutheran exile church, resolved in favor of the Missouri Synod. The archive's "independent of any diocese" is defensible in the narrow sense that LCMS polity is congregational (no bishops/dioceses) and the congregation owns its property, but "independent" overstates it. Also a naming refinement: the parish's actual name is Jerusalem Lutheran Church (Jeruzalės parapija), not simply "Lithuanian Lutheran Church." Everything else is consistent: founded 1903 by a knygnešys pastor (Martynas Keturakaitis, from the Tauragė region, imprisoned for book smuggling), still standing, still active, small and aging (avg. weekly attendance 21 in 2025 statistics).
gaps: The 2003 STLtoday centennial article is paywalled (HTTP 402); its details (Shimkus/ambassador visit, historical-landmark recognition, Jan 25 1903 organization date) come from search-result snippets of that article and could not be read in full. The church's Facebook page could not be read directly (login wall); the Feb 2025 post date comes from search results. No 120th-anniversary (2023) coverage was found. Degree of surviving Lithuanian identity in current worship is unverified beyond True Lithuania's note that by the 2000s only a third to half of the congregation was of Lithuanian heritage; no Lithuanian-language services are advertised. No press found on preservation campaigns, property transactions (none expected — the congregation has continuously owned the building), or LCMS district restructuring affecting it. Whether the archive's "Lithuanian Lutheran Church" is definitively this Jerusalem Lutheran congregation was cross-checked via the knygnešys-founder and Tauragė details, which match Keturakaitis exactly — the match is confident.
